questions.scenario: Your health check script produces output that must be saved to a log file for the on-call operator to review later.
questions.question: Write the output of 'echo HEALTH CHECK OK' to /tmp/health.log (overwrite if it exists).
questions.description: > redirects stdout to a file, overwriting it. Every shell has three standard streams: stdin (0), stdout (1), stderr (2). > is shorthand for 1>.
use_cases: Save script output to a log file
Create a file with specific content
Reset a log file at the start of each run
hints: Use the > operator after the command

questions.scenario: The health check runs every minute. Each result must be appended to the log, not overwrite it.
questions.question: Append 'CHECK COMPLETE' to /tmp/health.log without overwriting existing content.
questions.description: >> appends to a file instead of overwriting. If the file doesn't exist, >> creates it. Critical distinction from > in any logging script.
use_cases: Append entries to a rotating log
Add to an existing config file
Accumulate results across multiple script runs
hints: Unlike >, >> does not truncate the file first

questions.scenario: A backup script is producing errors you need to capture separately while discarding normal output.
questions.question: Run './scripts/backup.sh' sending stderr to /tmp/errors.log and discarding stdout.
questions.description: 2> redirects stderr (stream 2) to a file. > /dev/null silently discards stdout. /dev/null is a special file that swallows everything written to it.
use_cases: Capture only errors from noisy scripts
Suppress cron job stdout while preserving errors
Separate error logs from regular output
hints: Stderr is stream 2, redirect it with 2>

questions.scenario: You need to capture everything from a script — both stdout and stderr — into one combined log file.
questions.question: Run 'ls /home/student /nonexistent' and redirect both stdout AND stderr to /tmp/combined.log.
questions.description: 2>&1 redirects stderr into wherever stdout is currently going. Order matters: > file 2>&1 sends both to file. The reverse order (2>&1 > file) only redirects stderr to the original stdout.
use_cases: Capture all output from a cron job
Log complete script output including errors
Debug a script by capturing everything
hints: First redirect stdout to the file with >
Then redirect stderr to stdout with 2>&1

questions.scenario: A monitoring script runs a noisy command. You need it to run silently — no output at all, stdout or stderr.
questions.question: Run 'ls /home/student' discarding ALL output (both stdout and stderr).
questions.description: /dev/null is the null device — it discards everything. Redirecting both streams here gives you a completely silent command execution, useful in scripts where output would pollute logs.
use_cases: Suppress expected errors from commands used for their exit code
Run commands in cron without email spam
hints: Then redirect stderr there too with 2>&1

questions.scenario: You need to feed text directly into a command without creating a temporary file.
questions.question: Use a here-string to count the words in the string 'reactor block alpha online'.
questions.description: <<< (here-string) feeds a string directly into a command's stdin. It's cleaner than echo 'text' | command because it avoids spawning a subshell for echo.
use_cases: Feed a config value into a parsing command
Test a command with specific input without creating files
Pass a single-line string to read or awk
